policy.title: RBI Releases Final Guidelines on Climate Risk Management for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI mandates climate scenario analysis for all SCBs with assets over ₹1 lakh crore by March 2027, requiring banks to integrate physical and transition risk into ICAAP. Guidelines specify minimum two climate scenarios (orderly and disorderly transition) aligned with NGFS pathways.

policy.title: SEBI Extends BRSR Core to Top 500 Listed Companies with Enhanced Climate Metrics
policy.description: SEBI circular mandates BRSR Core reporting for top 500 companies by market cap from FY27, adding Scope 3 emissions for material categories and water stress intensity metrics. Reasonable assurance required for climate-related KPIs starting FY28.
policy.source: Securities and Exchange Board of India

policy.title: IRDAI Issues Exposure Draft on Index-Based Insurance Products for Climate Perils
policy.description: Draft guidelines permit general insurers to offer parametric covers triggered by pre-defined weather indices (rainfall deficit, temperature breach, wind speed). Products require certification of index correlation with actual losses exceeding 70% and mandatory basis risk disclosure.
policy.source: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India

policy.title: Basel Committee Finalizes Pillar 3 Climate Risk Disclosure Framework
policy.description: BCBS publishes final disclosure requirements effective January 2028, mandating banks to report climate-sensitive exposures by sector, geography, and maturity bucket. Includes standardized templates for physical risk concentration by hazard type.

policy.title: EFRAG Publishes Sector-Specific ESRS for Financial Institutions
policy.description: Sector standards require banks to disclose financed emissions using PCAF methodology and physical risk exposure by NACE sector. Non-EU subsidiaries of Indian banks operating in Europe will face reporting obligations from FY27.

policy.title: NGFS Releases Phase V Climate Scenarios with Enhanced Physical Risk Granularity
policy.description: Updated scenarios include country-level damage functions for India covering acute flooding, cyclones, and chronic heat stress. GDP impact estimates for India range from 3.2% (orderly) to 9.1% (hot house world) by 2050.
policy.source: Network for Greening the Financial System

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for Portfolio-Level Physical Risk Assessment
competitors.description: Jupiter will provide asset-level flood and cyclone risk scores for HDFC's commercial real estate portfolio across 12 Indian coastal cities. Partnership signals aggressive India market entry by global climate risk players.

competitors.title: GIST Advisory Closes $18M Series B to Expand Natural Capital Analytics
competitors.description: Round led by Omnivore Partners with participation from IFC. Funds will accelerate development of biodiversity risk scoring for Indian agri-lending portfolios and TNFD-aligned disclosure tools.

competitors.title: Moody's Launches India-Specific Climate Risk Adjusted PD Model
competitors.description: New model integrates district-level physical hazard data with corporate financials to generate climate-adjusted probability of default for Indian corporates. Available via API for integration into bank credit systems.

competitors.title: Sprih Launches BRSR Core Automation Platform with AI-Assisted Data Collection
competitors.description: Platform automates 65% of BRSR Core metrics through integration with ERP systems and utility APIs. Targets mid-cap listed companies facing new SEBI compliance deadlines.

competitors.title: XDI Signs Data Partnership with CRISIL for India Physical Risk Benchmarking
competitors.description: Partnership will create sector-level physical risk benchmarks for Indian industries using XDI's asset-level analytics. CRISIL will distribute risk scores through its existing bank relationships.

competitors.title: Watershed Raises $200M Series D at $3.5B Valuation
competitors.description: Funding will support expansion into financial services vertical with new financed emissions module. Company signals intention to enter Indian market through banking partnerships in 2026.

research.title: Climate Risk and Credit: Integrating Physical Hazards into Bank Lending Decisions in South Asia
research.description: Study of 340 Indian banks finds physical climate risk exposure could increase NPAs by 1.2-2.8 percentage points by 2035 under RCP 4.5. Recommends integrating hazard-adjusted LGD into pricing models for agriculture and real estate sectors.

research.title: Parametric Insurance for Climate Resilience: Lessons from Emerging Markets
research.description: Report identifies India's protection gap for climate perils at $28 billion annually. Finds parametric products reduce claims settlement time by 85% but require basis risk below 15% for commercial viability.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk Assessment: Methodological Guidance for Banks
research.description: Framework provides step-by-step methodology for banks to assess physical risk at portfolio level. Includes case studies from three Indian banks piloting asset-level hazard mapping for SME portfolios.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: Implications for Sustainable Finance
research.description: Analysis reveals 68% of Indian listed companies lack verified Scope 2 emissions data and 84% have no water risk disclosure. Recommends regulatory mandates and capacity building for data infrastructure.

research.title: Climate Scenario Analysis: Implementation Challenges for Indian Banks
research.description: Survey of 45 Indian banks finds only 12% have internal climate scenario analysis capabilities. Key barriers include lack of granular hazard data (cited by 78%) and absence of standardized damage functions for Indian sectors.

research.title: Index-Based Flood Insurance: Designing Products for Urban India
research.description: Technical paper proposes rainfall-runoff indices for 15 Indian metro areas calibrated against historical loss data. Finds optimal trigger design can achieve loss correlation of 78% for commercial property covers.

ourRead.headline: RBI's climate risk mandate creates a 12-month land grab for bank-grade hazard data—StepChange must lock in pilot clients before global players establish distribution moats.
body: This fortnight's signals converge on a single theme: Indian financial regulators are moving from guidance to mandate, and global players are positioning aggressively to capture the compliance-driven demand. The RBI's final climate risk guidelines, SEBI's BRSR Core expansion, and IRDAI's parametric exposure draft create overlapping deadlines that will force banks, insurers, and corporates to procure climate intelligence solutions within the next 18 months.

The RBI mandate (policy-1) is the pivotal development for StepChange's core business. The requirement for scenario analysis aligned with NGFS pathways by March 2027 means Indian SCBs need to operationalize physical risk assessment within 11 months. The CRISIL survey (research-5) confirms that 78% of banks cite granular hazard data as their primary barrier—this is precisely StepChange's capability gap to fill. The NGFS Phase V scenarios (policy-6) with India-specific damage functions provide the methodological backbone; StepChange should ensure its PD/LGD models align with these updated parameters before competitors can claim NGFS compliance.

The competitor signals demand urgent attention. Jupiter's HDFC partnership (comp-1) and XDI's CRISIL deal (comp-5) show global climate risk players are not waiting—they are building distribution through established Indian financial relationships. Moody's India-specific PD model (comp-3) directly competes with StepChange's core offering. However, these partnerships also validate the market. StepChange's advantage lies in deeper India hazard data and local regulatory understanding; the XDI-CRISIL partnership in particular suggests CRISIL may be open to data partnerships—worth exploring whether StepChange can offer competing or complementary arrangements with CRISIL's competitors like ICRA or CARE.

The IFC study (research-1) provides powerful sales ammunition: 1.2-2.8 percentage point NPA impact is a quantified risk that bank CFOs and CROs can act on. StepChange should integrate these findings into client presentations immediately. For parametric insurance, the Swiss Re report (research-2) establishes the 15% basis risk threshold as industry benchmark—StepChange's product design work must demonstrate performance below this threshold to achieve commercial credibility with insurers.

One thing to watch: whether IRDAI finalizes parametric guidelines before Q3 2026, which would accelerate insurer product development timelines. One action for the team: prioritize securing 2-3 pilot agreements with mid-tier private banks (assets ₹1-3 lakh crore) who face the March 2027 deadline but lack the internal resources to partner with global players—these are StepChange's natural first customers.
